summary:
The early phase of the Indus Valley Civilization, developing between approximately 3300 and 3000 BCE in the river valleys of what is now Pakistan and northwestern India, represents one of the world's four cradles of primary urban civilization. Also known as the Harappan Civilization after one of its major excavated cities, the Indus Valley tradition developed independently of Mesopotamia and Egypt, producing a sophisticated urban culture that remains incompletely understood due to the undeciphered state of the [[event:1683|Indus script]].

The earliest roots of the civilization trace back to the agricultural communities of Mehrgarh on the Balochistan plateau, occupied from roughly 7000 BCE onward. By the Early Harappan phase (3300-2600 BCE) that began during this era, settlements across the Indus plain and adjacent areas had grown significantly, craft specialization had increased, and long-distance trade networks were being established. Sites such as [[event:1682|Harappa]] and [[event:1681|Mohenjo-daro]] were already developing into proto-urban centers during this formative phase.

The physical environment of the Indus system — a vast alluvial plain watered by a network of rivers including the Indus and the now-largely-dried Ghaggar-Hakra (possibly the ancient Saraswati) — provided exceptional agricultural potential. Farmers cultivated wheat, barley, cotton, and sesame, while keeping cattle, sheep, goats, and possibly water buffalo. The reliable agricultural surplus generated by this system supported growing populations and enabled the specialization of labor that underlies urban complexity.

An unusual feature of the early Indus tradition is the relative standardization of material culture across a very large geographic area. Pottery styles, weights and measures, architectural conventions, and craft products show a remarkable consistency from distant sites, suggesting either strong trade connections, a shared cultural identity, or some form of administrative coordination that extended across the settlement network. This standardization became even more pronounced in the mature phase but is already visible in the Early Harappan period.

The Indus Civilization is notable for what the archaeological record does not easily reveal. Unlike Mesopotamia and Egypt, it has left no decipherable written texts describing rulers, wars, religious myths, or laws. The city layouts suggest a strong emphasis on sanitation and civic infrastructure — grid streets, standardized fired-brick construction, elaborate drainage systems — but the political and religious institutions that organized this effort remain largely opaque. No palaces or temples of the kind found in Mesopotamia have been identified with confidence, suggesting a governance model that may have been quite different from the divine kingship of Egypt or Sumer.

The early Indus phase set the foundations for the remarkable mature civilization that flourished after 2600 BCE, when cities like [[event:1681|Mohenjo-daro]] and Harappa reached their greatest extent and complexity. At its height the Indus Civilization covered an area larger than ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia combined, making it the most geographically extensive early civilization on Earth. Its legacy, though not fully understood, contributed to the deep cultural substrate of South Asian history.
